steps.title: Identify the goal: present the influence theory to an audience unfamiliar with the Haudenosaunee Confederacy
steps.content: The choice must (1) name or clearly identify the influence theory and (2) explain what it claims in terms an unfamiliar audience can follow — ideally without assuming prior knowledge of the Confederacy.

steps.title: Test each choice against the goal
steps.content:
• Choice A — Historian Laura Mitchell believes that the Great Law of Peace was very influential.: vague about what it influenced; never names or defines the influence theory. ✗
  • Choice B — The influence theory is supported by the fact that Benjamin Franklin and Thomas Jefferson both studied the Haudenosaunee Confederacy.: names the theory but only offers one supporting detail; does not explain what the theory actually claims. ✗
  • Choice C — The influence theory holds that the principles of the Great Law of Peace, a centuries-old agreement binding six Native nations in the northeastern US, influenced the US Constitution.: names the theory, states its claim (influenced the Constitution), and defines the Great Law of Peace in plain terms — everything an unfamiliar audience needs. ✓
  • Choice D — Native people, including the members of the Haudenosaunee Confederacy, influenced the founding of the US in many different ways.: too broad and vague; never identifies the influence theory or what it specifically claims. ✗
That matches Choice C — The influence theory holds that the principles of the Great Law of Peace, a centuries-old agreement binding six Native nations in the northeastern US, influenced the US Constitution..
